---
layout: post
title: "My top 5 EdTech tools for teaching"
date: 2014-09-13
blogger_url: "http://tjjteachersblog.blogspot.com/2014/09/my-top-5-edtech-tools-for-teaching.html"
---

<br/>

<img height="166" src="data:image/png;base64," width="320"/>

Today's @teachthought 30-day blogging challenge asks bloggers to list their to EdTech tools in terms of their effectiveness for teaching. Here's my list:

<ol>
<li>
<a href="https://realtimeboard.com/">RealtimeBoard</a> - I use this every lesson. It is amazing and I can't believe more people haven't caught on to it.
</li>
<li>
<a href="https://classroom.google.com/">Google classroom</a> - This is a bit of a cheat this because it's only just come out, but I'm already finding it's replacing <a href="https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/doctopus/ffhegaddkjpkfiemhhnphmnadfbkdhbf?hl=en">Doctopus</a> as my day-to-day homework management tool.
</li>
<li>
<a href="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google_Sites">Google sites</a> - Another offering from Google. Sites are easy to make and an excellent way of organising your teaching. <a href="https://sites.google.com/site/oswestrygeographydepartment/">Here's my site.</a>
</li>
<li>
<a href="http://www.gapminder.org/">Gapminder</a> - One for geographers, this. An invaluable tool for comparing nations. I haven't pointed pupils to the back of a paper atlas for years...

<!--more-->

</li>
<li>
<a href="http://www.census.gov/population/international/data/idb/informationGateway.php">IDB population pyramids</a> - Another one for geographers. Population pyramids for any country you like now, in the past and in the future. How did we ever manage beforehand?
</li>
</ol>

Bonus:

<a href="http://mapfight.appspot.com/">Mapfight</a> - Couldn't resist this, which I stumbled across a little while ago. It's a bit of fun and a great way to introduce a new country to a group.
